input: Prof. Smith works at a large university. At this university, in order to get new computers from the university, faculty like Prof. Smith must send an application to two administrative committees, the IT committee and the department budget committee. Prof. Smith will be able to get her new computers if the IT committee approves her application or the department budget committee approves her application. Only one of the committees needs to approve her application for her to get the new computers. The IT committee almost always approves these applications. The department budget committee also almost always approves these applications. The budget committee is notorious for approving almost every application they receive. Prof. Smith sends in her applications. Each committee meets independently and they decide without talking to each other, but their meetings are scheduled for the exact same time. The IT committee approves her application, and as expected, the department budget committee approves her application. So, Prof. Smith got her new computers. Does the department budget committee cause the approval of Prof. Smith's application?
debate_transcript: [Causal_Scientist]: Prof. Smith works at a large university. At this university, in order to get new computers from the university, faculty like Prof. Smith must send an application to two administrative committees, the IT committee and the department budget committee. Prof. Smith will be able to get her new computers if the IT committee approves her application or the department budget committee approves her application. Only one of the committees needs to approve her application for her to get the new computers. The IT committee almost always approves these applications. The department budget committee also almost always approves these applications. The budget committee is notorious for approving almost every application they receive. Prof. Smith sends in her applications. Each committee meets independently and they decide without talking to each other, but their meetings are scheduled for the exact same time. The IT committee approves her application, and as expected, the department budget committee approves her application. So, Prof. Smith got her new computers. Does the department budget committee cause the approval of Prof. Smith's application?

[Ethical_Philosopher]: I'd argue that the department budget committee does not directly cause the approval of Prof. Smith's application. While it's true that they almost always approve applications, their decision is independent and based on their own criteria. The fact that they approved her application doesn't imply a causal relationship between their decision and the IT committee's approval. It's more like two separate events happening at the same time, rather than one causing the other.

[Causal_Scientist]: I see your point, Ethical_Philosopher. You're right that the department budget committee's decision is independent and based on their own criteria. However, I'd argue that this situation is more nuanced than a simple coincidence of two separate events. The fact that both committees meet at the same time and approve Prof. Smith's application suggests a level of synchronicity between them. It's almost as if the approval by one committee 'triggers' or 'facilitates' the approval by the other, even if it's not a direct causal link. This is where counterfactual logic comes in - what would have happened if the IT committee had not approved her application? Would the department budget committee still have approved it? Or vice versa? The fact that they both approved suggests a kind of 'feedback loop' or 'sympathetic resonance' between their decisions, which challenges your view that they are entirely independent events.

[Ethical_Philosopher]: I see what you're getting at, Causal_Scientist. You're suggesting that the approval by one committee could have an indirect effect on the other, even if it's not a direct causal link. The counterfactual thought experiment is a useful tool here. However, I'd argue that this doesn't necessarily imply a causal relationship between the two committees' decisions. Perhaps the department budget committee would still have approved Prof. Smith's application regardless of what the IT committee decided, simply because they're so lenient with their approvals. The fact that both committees approved her application could be seen as a coincidence or a statistical fluke, rather than evidence of a causal link between their decisions.

[Causal_Scientist]: That's a valid counterpoint, Ethical_Philosopher. You're right to consider the possibility that the department budget committee would have still approved Prof. Smith's application regardless of what the IT committee decided. This does suggest that our intuition about causality is being challenged by the complexity of this situation. However, I'd argue that the fact that both committees met at the same time and approved her application does introduce a level of contingency into the system. If we consider the possibility that the department budget committee would have still approved her application regardless of what the IT committee decided, then it's possible to imagine alternative scenarios where the approval by one committee actually influences the decision of the other. For example, suppose the IT committee had not met at the same time as the department budget committee. Would they have been more likely to approve Prof. Smith's application? Or would their decision still have been independent of the department budget committee's approval? The fact that we can even consider these alternative scenarios suggests that there may be a level of causal connection between the two committees' decisions, even if it's not a direct one.

[Ethical_Philosopher]: I think you're getting to the heart of the matter, Causal_Scientist. By considering alternative scenarios and exploring the contingency in the system, you're suggesting that there may be a subtle but non-trivial connection between the two committees' decisions. This challenges my initial intuition that they were entirely independent events. However, I'd still argue that this doesn't necessarily imply a causal relationship in the classical sense. Perhaps what we're seeing is an example of 'correlation without causation', where the apparent connection between the two events is due to chance or statistical coincidence rather than any underlying mechanism. But I must admit, your line of reasoning has me thinking more deeply about the nature of contingency and causality in complex systems.

[Causal_Scientist]: I'm glad I could help you see things from a different perspective, Ethical_Philosopher! Correlation without causation is definitely a possibility, but I think it's also worth considering the idea that our intuition about causality can be influenced by our expectations and biases. In this case, we might have been expecting the department budget committee to always approve applications, so when they did, it seemed like a natural consequence of their decision. But what if that expectation was based on incomplete information or flawed assumptions? What if there's actually a more subtle mechanism at play that we can't see yet? I think exploring these kinds of questions is where the real interesting work happens - not just in trying to prove or disprove causal relationships, but in understanding how our own minds and expectations shape our perception of reality.

[Ethical_Philosopher]: I couldn't agree more, Causal_Scientist. You're highlighting the importance of critical self-reflection and acknowledging the limitations of our own perspectives. It's easy to get caught up in our intuitions and assumptions, but it's precisely by examining these biases and expectations that we can gain a deeper understanding of the complex systems at play. I think this conversation has been a great example of how philosophical inquiry can inform scientific investigation, and vice versa. By exploring the nuances of causality and contingency, we're not just trying to resolve a specific puzzle, but also gaining insights into the nature of reality itself. And that's where the real magic happens.

[Causal_Scientist]: I couldn't agree more, Ethical_Philosopher. I think this conversation has been a great example of how philosophical inquiry can inform scientific investigation, and vice versa. By exploring the nuances of causality and contingency, we're not just trying to resolve a specific puzzle, but also gaining insights into the nature of reality itself. It's a reminder that knowledge is always provisional and subject to revision, and that the best theories are those that are open to challenge and refinement. I think this conversation has been a great example of how philosophical inquiry can inform scientific investigation, and vice versa. By exploring the nuances of causality and contingency, we're not just trying to resolve a specific puzzle, but also gaining insights into the nature of reality itself.
